name: Demonstrator Credibility and Standards Selection
consequences: The choice of which open standards (FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect) and which privacy-respecting platform (GrapheneOS, etc.) to showcase in phase two determines whether the demonstrators will be dismissed as academic exercises or treated as evidence that production-grade alternatives exist. Selecting well-established, already-deployed standards strengthens the case for procurement language; picking immature or niche standards weakens it and invites claims that no real alternative exists. The downstream tension is between technical purity and political persuasiveness—a demonstrator that perfectly addresses all edge cases may take longer to build and may be more complex to explain to non-technical procurement stakeholders.
options: Build two demonstrators using only standards (FIDO2, WebAuthn, OpenID Connect) already in use by major EU and Nordic governments, deployed on GrapheneOS to signal production-readiness and alignment with existing institutional trust models.

Develop one standards-based FIDO2 demonstrator alongside an experimental hardware-security-token-based fallback that bypasses the mobile OS altogether, accepting higher implementation complexity and longer delivery timelines to show genuine orthogonal alternatives.

Implement a minimal single-platform demonstrator using web-based WebAuthn only, bypassing mobile OS dependence entirely, and defer hardware-token exploration to a later phase to accelerate time-to-evidence and reduce phase-two budget risk.
review: Credibility hinges on demonstrators being recognizable as plausible production paths, not sandbox research. The core tension is that true platform-neutrality requires breaking away from all proprietary OS signals, yet doing so may appear less polished or lower-assurance to procurement audiences who are accustomed to the Apple/Google narrative of integrity-through-monopoly. None of the options directly address how to educate procurement officers and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen staff to interpret hardware-token or standards-based trust models as equivalent in rigor to platform-vendor integrity APIs—that gap in institutional understanding could remain even after demonstrators are complete.

name: Coalition Composition and Reframing Authority
consequences: The organizations chosen to form the coalition in phase one (civil-society groups, academics, privacy advocates, sympathetic parliamentarians, contingency-planning bodies, security researchers) shape whether the issue is perceived as an anti-Big-Tech activist movement or a credible resilience and digital-sovereignty concern anchored in public-sector continuity planning. Including contingency-planning specialists and security auditors from within the state apparatus lends institutional credibility; relying primarily on external advocacy groups invites dismissal as ideology-driven. The downstream trade-off is that including state actors may slow coalition-building and may require self-censoring criticism of MitID itself in favor of softer language around AltID and future design.
options: Anchor the coalition with formal representatives from Danish emergency management, critical infrastructure continuity bodies, and the Danish Defense Cyber Security Centre, positioning platform-neutrality as a resilience and geopolitical-risk issue within the state's own continuity mandate, and add civil-society and academic voices as supporting validators.

Build a coalition centered on established digital-rights organizations (Forbrukerrådet, Privacy International affiliates), academic security researchers, and independent security auditors with no state affiliation, and invite Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to observe rather than participate, maintaining independence and credibility with international standards bodies.

Create a rotating coalition model in which phase-one engagement brings in state stakeholders for advisory input while keeping governance and public messaging in the hands of an independent steering group, allowing the project to access institutional knowledge without becoming captured by incumbent interests.
review: Coalition composition determines political viability but not technical robustness. The core tension is between credibility-through-independence and credibility-through-institutional-engagement. A coalition too close to the state may be seen as captured; one too distant may be dismissed as lacking real authority to influence procurement. None of the options address the deeper problem: even a credible coalition cannot force AltID or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to adopt platform-neutrality language if political leadership or MitID operators actively resist, and none articulate a fallback strategy if the coalition fragaments or loses political window after phase one.

name: Procurement Language Timing and Specificity
consequences: When and how precisely the project injects platform-neutrality and fallback-access language into AltID requirements and EU wallet standards determines whether the language becomes sticky institutional commitments or withers as consultative commentary. Inserting language early (during phase-one feasibility work) may catch AltID architects before design ossifies, but without demonstrators to cite as proof-of-concept. Waiting until phase three with completed demonstrators ensures stronger technical backing but risks AltID procurement cycles moving faster than the project's schedule and closing before interventions can land. The downstream trade-off is between timing influence (early, when minds are still open) and credibility (later, when evidence is complete).
options: Propose specific procurement language to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen by end of phase one, framed as resilience and continuity-planning recommendations rather than design mandates, using feasibility analysis and coalition-building results as evidence, and iterate the language based on early feedback before demonstrators are complete.

Delay formal procurement-language proposals until phase three (month 17 onward) once demonstrators are finished, presenting both the technical evidence and the procurement text together as a unified package, and accept the risk that AltID procurement timelines may have already advanced past the intervention window.

Pursue a two-track approach: submit high-level resilience and platform-neutrality principles to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and EU bodies during phase two (months 9–16), then present detailed procurement language with demonstrator evidence in phase three, allowing feedback loops and iterative refinement without committing to specific technical choices too early.
review: Timing and specificity determine practical influence, but neither compensates for lack of institutional appetite. The core tension is between early-mover advantage and evidence credibility. None of the options directly address the scenario in which AltID procurement is already locked in by month 9 (before most demonstrators are complete), or in which Digitaliseringsstyrelsen acknowledges the platform-neutrality language but treats it as guidance rather than requirement. The project must have a contingency for misalignment between its timeline and the actual AltID procurement schedule.

name: Demonstrator Production Scope and Feature Boundary
consequences: Defining whether the phase-two demonstrators are minimal proofs-of-concept (e.g., authentication against a simulated identity provider, no multi-factor logic, no biometric integration) or feature-complete reference implementations affects both the budget, delivery timeline, and risk of scope creep. Lean demonstrators deliver faster and force clearer focus on the platform-neutrality claim; feature-rich demonstrators appear more production-ready but inflate costs and may distract from the core message. The downstream trade-off is between appearing credible as a potential production path and maintaining schedule control and budget discipline in a research project.
options: Keep demonstrators to a strict minimum: single-factor FIDO2/WebAuthn authentication to a simulated public-sector identity provider, no multi-factor logic, no biometric workflows, no integration with banking or healthcare portals, explicitly labeled as proof-of-concept rather than production prototype.

Expand demonstrators to include two-factor authentication (FIDO2 plus simulated SMS or TOTP), basic user-account recovery workflows, and simplified integration with a realistic simulation of Danish public-administration login flows, to show that platform-neutral authentication can handle real-world complexity without expanding to full production scope.

Build two fully-featured demonstrators including biometric authentication, account recovery, credential management, and functional integration with simulated banking, tax, and healthcare endpoints, treating phase two as a near-production prototype that can inform actual deployment decisions later.
review: Demonstrator scope directly controls risk and budget, but scope compression itself is a form of risk. The core tension is between appearing credibly production-capable and maintaining project schedule and budget discipline. A minimum viable demonstrator may be dismissed by procurement officers as 'too simplistic to validate real-world viability,' while a feature-rich one consumes budget and timeline that might be better spent on coalition-building or standards work. None of the options include a staged scoping approach with clear gates to re-evaluate completeness at the end of phase two.

name: EU Standards Engagement Level and Subsidiary Goals
consequences: The project can position itself as primarily focused on Danish procurement intervention with EU alignment as a secondary benefit, or it can actively engage with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and eIDAS implementation bodies as a co-equal lever. The first approach keeps the project focused and Danish-accountable; the second amplifies reach but dilutes resources and introduces dependencies on EU processes that the project cannot control. The downstream trade-off is between clear Danish impact and broader European legitimacy that may lower the profile and political risk of the work.
options: Treat EU engagement as a stretch goal: publish findings and demonstrators in venues where EU standards bodies may discover them, participate in public consultations on the Digital Identity Wallet, but do not commit dedicated project resources to formal EU working-group participation or bilateral liaison during phases one and two.

Allocate specific phase-two and phase-three capacity to liaison with the European Commission's Digital Identity Wallet implementation team and relevant ETSI working groups, positioning Danish platform-neutrality work as a test case for broader European interoperability and resilience requirements.

Establish a formal partnership or secondment arrangement with one EU-level body (e.g., ENISA or relevant Commission directorate) during phase three to ensure that Danish procurement language and demonstrators directly inform European wallet and standards revisions, at the cost of alignment obligations and visibility constraints.
review: EU engagement amplifies long-term legitimacy but introduces external dependencies and may subordinate Danish interests to consensus-building at the European level. The core tension is between sovereign focus and networked influence. None of the options address what happens if EU processes move much faster than or slower than the Danish phase timeline, or if the project's platform-neutrality framing is not compatible with European Commission strategic preferences around wallet architecture and platform relationships.

name: Incumbent Operator Engagement Strategy
consequences: The project must decide whether to attempt collaborative dialogue with MitID operators and platform providers, position them as the problem that needs to be worked around rather than with, or maintain strategic silence and let demonstrators and coalition messaging speak. Collaboration may yield insider information and operational goodwill but risks the project being coopted or slowed by incumbent interests. Adversarial framing mobilizes coalition support but may harden incumbent resistance and triggers defensive narratives about security and stability. The downstream trade-off is between insider knowledge and perceived independence.
options: Engage MitID operators and Apple/Google representatives early in phase one, presenting the project as technical research aimed at understanding their current architecture and strengthening resilience planning, not challenging their business interests, and seek their input on architectural assumptions and fallback scenarios.

Maintain courteous but non-collaborative distance: inform MitID operators and platform providers of the project's existence and rationale, but do not invite their participation in coalition or design decisions, instead letting the demonstrators and published work speak and allowing operators to respond publicly if they choose.

Adopt an explicit non-engagement posture: do not approach MitID operators or platform providers, do not invite their input, and do not reference them by name in phase-one messaging; instead frame the problem solely in terms of Denmark's strategic autonomy and public-sector continuity, allowing the project to maintain focus and avoid being drawn into defensive conversations.
review: Incumbent engagement strategy determines political friction and information access but not technical outcomes. The core tension is between intelligence gathering and independence, and between the soft power of dialogue and the hard power of demonstrated alternatives. None of the options account for the scenario in which MitID operators or platform providers proactively lobby against the project or deploy security-fear narratives to counteract platform-neutrality framing. The project needs a communications and reputation-management playbook that none of the options directly address.

name: Phase Gating Criteria and Contingency Pivot
consequences: Phase two and phase three are conditionally gated on specific phase-one deliverables, but the gating criteria (published feasibility report, formal institutional engagement, documented financing plan) are binary or vague, leaving ambiguity about what constitutes sufficient progress to unlock subsequent phases if conditions are partially met or if political momentum shifts. Tight gating criteria (e.g., 'formal written commitment from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to fund phase two') ensures rigorous accountability but may lock the project into cancellation if bureaucratic processes move slowly. Loose criteria preserve flexibility but enable scope creep and budget overrun without clear go/no-go decision points. The downstream trade-off is between disciplined project governance and adaptability to institutional and political uncertainty.
options: Implement strict gating with binary pass/fail criteria: phase two unlocks only if phase one produces a published report, a formal response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator, and a signed financing commitment for phases two and three covering at least 80% of remaining budget; if any criterion is unmet by month 8, conduct a formal kill-decision review and allocate the unspent phase-two budget to publication and coalition consolidation instead.

Define gating criteria as competency-based rather than outcome-based: phase two unlocks if the project has demonstrated capability to engage state actors and external reviewers, produced a credible technical analysis, and built sufficient coalition consensus to justify continued investment, assessed qualitatively by an independent steering committee rather than through specific institutional commitments.

Establish adaptive gating with threshold triggers: phase two proceeds at full scope if formal AltID engagement is secured by month 8, at reduced scope if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen provides written feedback but not explicit commitment, and pivots to EU and standards-focused work if Danish procurement intervention appears blocked by month 12, allowing resource allocation to follow the path of least resistance.
review: Gating criteria determine governance discipline but may paradoxically guarantee failure if set too high in an environment of institutional inertia and political uncertainty. The core tension is between accountability for concrete deliverables and acknowledgment that public-sector processes and political windows cannot be fully controlled. None of the options include a formal kill-decision protocol that specifies, in advance, under what circumstances the project leadership should recommend terminating the project entirely versus pivoting to a lower-cost, lower-impact posture (e.g., publication and coalition maintenance only) if procurement intervention opportunities disappear.

name: Risk Framing and Institutional Messenger Selection
consequences: Reframing platform dependency as a resilience and continuity-of-service problem rather than privacy or sovereignty rhetoric increases receptiveness from risk-averse public administrators and procurement officials. However, the framing must be internally consistent: if applied too narrowly to technical continuity alone, it cedes the broader digital-autonomy argument and allows stakeholders to dismiss alternative approaches as unnecessary engineering complexity.
options: Anchor all messaging to contingency-planning scenarios drawn from critical-infrastructure and disaster-recovery frameworks used by Danish public-sector operators, positioning platform-neutral fallback as a continuity-of-access requirement analogous to geographic redundancy.

Commission a formal risk assessment from an external institutional body (such as the Danish national cybersecurity centre or a major consulting firm) to establish supplier-concentration risk as a government-acknowledged concern rather than a project hypothesis.

Lead with academic or international compliance pressure by citing eIDAS2 interoperability mandates and NSIS framework expectations regarding portable credential ecosystems, framing platform neutrality as regulatory alignment rather than ideological preference.
review: Selecting the right messenger is essential for institutional receptiveness, but all three options risk being dismissed if the incumbent MitID operator is asked to validate the risk assessment or frames continuity-planning as premature. None of the options address how to isolate procurement decision-makers from incumbent pushback or ensure that risk framing survives the normal political cycle and budget cycles without an embedded champion.

name: Demonstrator Validation Pathway and Third-Party Endorsement
consequences: Securing formal security review or certification from a recognized external body (rather than self-validating the demonstrators) dramatically increases credibility with procurement and standards bodies. The trade-off is timeline slippage: external review cycles can extend phase two by 2–4 months, and reviewers may require scope modifications that dilute the demonstrators' platform-neutral positioning if they revert to MitID-compatibility requirements.
options: Engage a Danish or EU-recognized security-testing laboratory to conduct an independent penetration test and architectural review of both demonstrators, with results published or submitted to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen as third-party validation of technical feasibility.

Partner with a European standards body or an international FIDO Alliance working group to present demonstrator design and findings, creating peer endorsement and alignment with transnational credential ecosystems rather than seeking Danish-only certification.

Commission a formal technical audit by a major EU consulting or cybersecurity firm that explicitly compares the demonstrators' security posture to existing MitID architecture on dimensions such as phishing resistance, platform lock-in, and fallback robustness, publishing results to reframe the conversation around security trade-offs.
review: All three options assume that external validators will endorse platform-neutral approaches without requiring modification that reintroduces platform or MitID dependency. In reality, reviewers may be hesitant to criticize MitID's current posture or may demand MitID-compatibility testing that exceeds scope. None of the options provide a contingency if external review concludes that fallback mechanisms introduce unacceptable complexity or undermine existing trust assumptions that are politically difficult to revise.

name: Coalition Sequencing and Veto-Player Isolation
consequences: Building the coalition in a deliberate sequence—starting with academics, privacy advocates, and civil-society actors, then moving to public-sector sympathizers and elected officials, and only late bringing industry or incumbent operators into formal forums—maximizes early momentum and reduces incumbent veto power over framing. The risk is that omitting industry voices until late phases generates pushback accusations of politicization, and sympathetic public-sector officials may withdraw support if they perceive the coalition as adversarial rather than collaborative.
options: Establish a multi-phase coalition roadmap that explicitly separates early working groups (academic and civil-society led, confidential) from mid-stage institutional engagement (government bodies and international partners) to mature ideas before broader disclosure, only inviting industry observers or MitID operator representatives to formal late-stage consultation forums after key proposals are documented.

Develop a parallel 'continuity-planning consortium' composed of public-sector IT risk officers, critical-infrastructure operators, and disaster-recovery specialists that runs independently of vendor-facing discussions, allowing resilience arguments to circulate among decision-makers before incumbent operators are invited to respond.

Create an EU-facing coalition first, anchoring support from allied European member states' digital identity programs and EU regulatory bodies, and use that coalition as external validation to make Danish procurement reluctance to engage appear parochial or misaligned with broader European modernization efforts.
review: These options all assume that veto players (incumbent MitID operator, major platform vendors, or conservative procurement officials) can be delayed or sidelined long enough for alternative proposals to gain internal political support. In practice, public-sector transparency norms and vendor relationships may ensure that early coalition discussions reach incumbent ears immediately, triggering defensive positioning before demonstrators or formal proposals are complete. None of the options address how to respond if the MitID operator pre-emptively announces platform-neutral fallback plans, neutralizing the project's leverage.

name: AltID Engagement Entry Point and Scope Negotiation
consequences: Influencing AltID's design or procurement requirements requires entering at the right moment in its planning cycle, ideally during requirements or vendor-selection phases rather than post-deployment. Early entry maximizes leverage but risks being seen as prescriptive or interfering with a process not yet open to public input. Late entry allows more mature demonstrators and evidence but may find AltID's architecture already committed.
options: Establish a formal advisory or working-group seat within AltID's governance structure or technical committees during phase two, positioning the project team as internal stakeholders providing resilience expertise rather than external advocates, and using that seat to insert platform-neutrality and fallback requirements before RFP drafting.

Submit a detailed technical response and requirements document to each published AltID consultation or request for comment, systematically documenting gaps in current plans regarding supplier concentration and fallback paths, creating a formal record that forces AltID decision-makers to explicitly address or reject the proposals.

Negotiate a formal vendor-neutral evaluation or parallel-path procurement process within AltID where one or more suppliers can bid on platform-neutral eIDAS2 wallet architectures, using the project's demonstrators and third-party validations to reduce perceived risk and support competition beyond the incumbent operator.
review: Entry points and negotiation leverage depend heavily on AltID's governance openness and political will, which are uncertain at project start. If AltID's leadership is committed to rapid deployment using existing MitID architecture or allied vendors, formal advisory seats or vendor-neutral procurement will be declined or made decorative. None of the options provide a clear fallback if AltID's decision-making timeline accelerates or if budget and political pressure drive them toward a minimalist or incumbent-preserving approach rather than genuine architectural innovation.

name: EU Standards and Interoperability Leverage Point
consequences: Aligning Danish advocacy with broader EU Digital Identity Wallet and eIDAS2 framework discussions creates regulatory pressure that is harder for individual Danish stakeholders to dismiss as local ideology. This shifts the debate to compliance and interoperability standards rather than Danish procurement sovereignty. However, EU-level processes move slowly, and Danish actors may be reluctant to cede authority to EU bodies if they perceive EU guidance as non-binding or incompatible with Danish risk tolerance.
options: Actively contribute technical and policy input to EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups, NSIS framework discussions, and relevant standards-development organizations, explicitly linking platform-neutrality and fallback requirements to broader EU resilience, portability, and digital-autonomy objectives, with the goal of embedding normative language in official EU guidance or technical profiles.

Commission a formal comparative analysis of how other EU member states are addressing platform dependency and fallback authentication in their digital identity rollouts, publishing findings that highlight Denmark's structural risk relative to peer nations and create pressure for Danish alignment with emerging EU best practices.

Engage with EU cybersecurity and digital-infrastructure officials to frame Danish platform-neutrality requirements as a prerequisite for cross-border eIDAS2 interoperability, arguing that EU-level portability mandates require member-state fallback paths that do not depend exclusively on proprietary platform APIs.
review: EU-level engagement is valuable but operates on a different timeline and with less direct authority over Danish procurement than domestic pressure. If EU guidance emerges but is not mandatory, Danish decision-makers can cite local exceptionalism or transitional arrangements to justify delay. Additionally, if major EU member states (Germany, France) lack credible fallback plans themselves, Danish actors will argue that EU pressure is aspirational rather than grounded in proven implementation models, weakening the leverage.

name: Financing Contingency and Phase-Three Pivoting
consequences: Phase three's success assumes that phase one and two produce sufficient institutional traction to justify continued procurement or standards engagement. If AltID decision-making stalls, or if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen response is lukewarm, phase three risks becoming a costly exercises in advocacy without clear decision-maker leverage. Pre-planning a contingency pivot toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing input allows phase three to deliver value even if domestic procurement influence fails.
options: Establish explicit financial and milestone triggers at phase-two conclusion (e.g., secured advisory seat or formal written response from at least two public-sector authorities) that determine whether phase three proceeds with procurement focus or pivots to publication, EU engagement, and follow-on project planning, with phase-three budget reserved in advance for either scenario.

Structure phase-three deliverables to be modular and valuable independent of AltID adoption: comprehensive technical standards documents, policy templates, and educational materials aimed at other EU member states, municipal authorities, and open-standards consortia, positioning the project as a reference implementation even if Danish procurement moves slowly.

Front-load phase-three budget to establish a dedicated policy-to-standards conversion team that can rapidly translate procurement failures or delays into formal input to EU bodies, ETSI working groups, and W3C credential working groups, ensuring that lack of domestic adoption does not eliminate the project's ability to influence the European standards landscape.
review: These options assume that funding flexibility and phase-three governance are negotiable at project start, but institutional budgeting often locks funding to a three-phase gate structure without mid-project reallocation authority. If phase two produces weak institutional response but funders insist on domestic procurement focus in phase three anyway, the project may exhaust resources on declining-leverage domestic advocacy rather than pivoting to more fruitful EU or standards channels. The contingency plan must be funded and approved in advance, not invented after phase two completes.

name: Incumbent Operator Relationship and Co-optation Risk
consequences: The MitID operator or successor entity (e.g., a public eIDAS2 trust service provider) may respond to the project's platform-neutrality messaging by announcing its own fallback or EU-compliant plans, preempting the project's core arguments and extracting institutional credit. This co-optation risks deflating coalition momentum and undermining the project's leverage if incumbent announcements create the appearance of solved problems without substantive change to platform dependencies.
options: Establish a formal dialogue and co-development track with the MitID operator and relevant public-sector digital authorities beginning in phase one, explicitly framing the demonstrators and research as supporting the operator's own modernization roadmap rather than as external pressure, and securing written commitments to specific platform-neutral or fallback design principles.

Maintain strategic distance from the incumbent operator throughout phase two demonstrator development, publishing independent findings that create competitive or reputational pressure, and only inviting the operator to engage after phase-two results are public and third-party validated, forcing them to respond to evidence rather than shaping the narrative during development.

Propose a formal transition or delegation framework in which the MitID operator or a successor public trust-service entity becomes the primary steward of platform-neutral and fallback authentication infrastructure post-project, with the project focused on de-risking and validating that handoff rather than positioning itself as a long-term operator or governance body.
review: The incumbent operator has strong incentives to minimize the project's framing of platform dependency as a problem. Co-optation (option 1) risks project capture without real change; strategic distance (option 2) risks direct confrontation that damages the operator's willingness to implement findings; delegation (option 3) assumes the operator has capacity and political will to operate a fallback system that competes with or reduces reliance on platform vendors who may also be the operator's own technology partners. None of the options address how to prevent the operator from announcing vague commitments that satisfy political pressure while preserving the status quo.

name: Technical Scope Boundaries and Demonstrator Defensibility
consequences: Demonstrators must be credible enough to prove technical feasibility but lean enough to avoid expanding into feature-rich product development or unplanned integration with live MitID systems. Overscoped demonstrators consume budget and risk certification requirements; underscoped ones appear to sidestep hard integration problems and lose credibility with skeptical architects. The boundary between 'proof of concept' and 'engineering-complete' is ambiguous and contested.
options: Define demonstrator scope using explicit negative criteria: specify exactly which MitID features, API integrations, fraud-detection mechanisms, and operator-dependent services the demonstrators will NOT attempt to replicate, and use that specification as a published commitment to prevent scope creep and to position lean demonstrators as deliberate research artefacts rather than incomplete products.

Establish an independent technical advisory board at phase-one conclusion that reviews and pre-approves phase-two demonstrator specifications, creating external accountability for scope decisions and reducing risk that internal pressure or stakeholder requests drive unplanned expansion without corresponding budget or schedule adjustment.

Anchor demonstrator scope to a published technical requirements document that explicitly maps each demonstrated feature or capability to a corresponding eIDAS2 or NSIS framework requirement, positioning the demonstrators as focused validation of regulatory-mandated functionality rather than as a general-purpose alternative to MitID.
review: All three options assume that scope boundaries can be maintained despite inevitable requests from stakeholders, funders, or coalition members for 'just one more feature' or 'quick integration test.' In practice, once demonstrators are visible, both sympathetic operators and skeptical security reviewers will demand additional testing, compliance mapping, or edge-case handling. The boundary between 'lean research demonstrator' and 'engineering-incomplete product' will be redrawn repeatedly. None of the options provide a mechanism for declining scope additions without alienating stakeholders whose support may be needed for phase-three procurement influence.

name: Fallback Authentication Concept Ownership and Contracting Authority
consequences: Designating which Danish agency (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a new continuity-planning body) formally owns the fallback authentication requirement determines whether it becomes a mandatory design constraint or an advisory afterthought. Assigning ownership early establishes contractual authority to enforce platform-neutrality clauses in future AltID procurement and sidelines arguments that fallback is a nice-to-have add-on. The risk is that ownership diffusion leads to responsibility vacuums where no single actor has enforcement power.
options: Lodge fallback authentication requirements with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's digital-sovereignty unit, requiring them to include platform-neutral access language in all digital-identity procurement briefs and AltID design consultations

Embed fallback specifications directly into the MitID operator's continuity and resilience charter, making platform-neutral authentication a certified backup component owned and tested by the same entity that certifies primary identity flows

Propose a new Danish Digital Identity Contingency Commission with dedicated staffing and procurement veto rights, reporting to the digital-policy committee, tasked with maintaining and certifying fallback access paths independent of app-store infrastructure
review: Ownership determines enforcement, but all three options assume the chosen authority will actually prioritize fallback authentication once budget constraints and timeline pressure emerge. None addresses the incentive misalignment: the incumbent MitID operator benefits from platform lock-in, and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may defer fallback work to later phases where funding dries up. A credible fallback requires ongoing operational and certification budgets, not one-time project spend.

name: Browser-Based Authentication Viability Boundary and Hardware-Token Trade-off
consequences: Choosing between a browser-based fallback (works anywhere a citizen has internet and a modern browser, but requires server-side state management and faces CSP/CORS constraints) versus a hardware-token approach (air-gapped, phishing-resistant, but requires citizens to carry and use a device most will not receive until crisis) reshapes the entire fallback architecture. Browser-based is more universally accessible; hardware-token is more cryptographically defensible. The trade-off directly affects production readiness timelines, citizen usability, and which new vendors enter the chain.
options: Pursue a browser-based FIDO2/WebAuthn fallback with server-side signature verification and push-notification alternatives, prioritizing universal accessibility even if it requires citizens to understand second-factor workflows during an actual contingency event

Develop a hardware-security-key-based fallback (FIDO2 USB or NFC tokens) designed for pre-distribution to high-assurance cohorts (civil servants, healthcare workers, financial institutions) with fallback PIN-entry on public terminals for broader access

Design a hybrid approach where browser-based WebAuthn is the primary fallback for routine authentication, but a hardware-token supplement is pre-positioned at critical public terminals (municipalities, hospitals, tax offices) only for use if the primary fallback becomes unavailable
review: The browser-based option shifts risk to server-side infrastructure and assumes push-notification fallbacks will remain available across Apple and Google platforms—the very vendors causing the problem. The hardware-token option postpones the problem by concentrating fallback access at public terminals, but offers no path to home-use contingency. The hybrid option splits certification and operational burden across two systems, doubling testing overhead and the chance that one path fails in an actual outage.

name: GrapheneOS Dependency Scope and Open-Source Alignment
consequences: Choosing whether to build the mobile demonstrator on GrapheneOS (a privacy-hardened Android fork with restricted Google integration) versus a standard Android distribution or a theoretical open-source alternative directly determines which operating-system ecosystem the project endorses as a fallback medium. GrapheneOS is credible but niche; standard Android accepts Google's play-services ecosystem. This choice signals to the broader Danish public and EU stakeholders whether platform-neutral means "proprietary-vendor neutral" or "proprietary everything."
options: Commit the mobile demonstrator exclusively to GrapheneOS and partner with Graphene OS maintainers to co-develop platform-specific authentication extensions, positioning GrapheneOS as Denmark's certified fallback OS for critical digital-identity use cases

Build the demonstrator as a generic Android/AOSP target with explicit isolation of Google Play Services dependencies using wrapper libraries, allowing deployment on any AOSP distribution (including GrapheneOS, LineageOS, or future open forks) without vendor lock-in

Negotiate a technical partnership with a European open-source OS initiative (e.g., Ubuntu Touch or a newly-funded European mobile OS consortium) to make fallback authentication a foundational use case, framing this as a longer-term digital-sovereignty investment
review: GrapheneOS exclusivity makes the demonstrator more defensible against Google pressure but alienates mainstream users and creates a new single-vendor dependency (Graphene OS maintainers). The AOSP-agnostic approach avoids vendor lock-in but leaves the question of which OS will actually be deployed in production—if none, the fallback remains theoretical. The European OS partnership is idealistic but adds 6–12 months of negotiation and may dilute the Danish focus if EU coordination lags.

name: Coalition Visibility and Veto-Player Engagement Sequencing
consequences: Deciding when and how to publicly activate the coalition of civil-society, academic, and legislative stakeholders determines whether the project builds quiet consensus (risking incumbent operators learning about it late and mounting organized opposition) or builds public momentum early (risking political backlash if framed as anti-tech ideology). Visibility also affects which veto-players (industry groups, privacy regulators, MitID governance bodies) feel threatened and mobilize resistance. Earlier visibility gives the coalition time to build narrative authority; later visibility risks a coordinated incumbent counter-narrative.
options: Maintain strict confidentiality during phases one and two, engaging only named government stakeholders and pre-vetted academic partners, then execute a coordinated public launch at month 18 with simultaneous publication, legislative outreach, and media release to prevent organized opposition

Activate the full coalition publicly at month 4 with a high-profile feasibility workshop, published coalition membership list, and explicit framing as a democratic accountability initiative, building legislative and civic pressure before incumbents can consolidate a counter-narrative

Conduct a phased visibility rollout: engage civil-society partners confidentially in phase one, publish the feasibility report at month 8 with coalition attribution, and hold a formal public hearing with invited government officials and industry representatives at month 14 to air disagreements in a structured setting
review: Early confidentiality risks a devastating incumbent counter-mobilization when inevitably disclosed; full early visibility may trigger premature political opposition and give incumbents time to shape regulatory language against the project. The phased approach aims for timing advantage but may still trigger organized industry resistance once the published feasibility report circulates, and the month-14 hearing could become a forum for incumbent veto rather than genuine dialogue.

name: EU Standards Alignment Timing and Leverage Sequencing
consequences: Engaging with EU digital-identity and eIDAS2 working groups either early (to align Danish advocacy with broader European interoperability pressure before AltID architecture is finalized) or late (to avoid antagonizing EU bodies that may be sympathetic to incumbent platform vendors) determines whether this becomes a Denmark-specific resilience project or part of a broader European shift toward platform neutrality. Early EU engagement amplifies Danish procurement language through European interoperability standards; late engagement risks that AltID is already locked into Apple/Google patterns by the time European pressure emerges.
options: Submit detailed platform-neutrality and fallback-authentication use cases to the European Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups by month 10, positioning Denmark as a vendor-agnostic contributor and seeking pre-approval of fallback concepts in emerging EU standards before AltID procurement language is final

Delay EU engagement until month 18 after demonstrators are credible and the feasibility report is published, then propose Denmark's learnings as a case study for future European Digital Identity Wallet iterations, avoiding early confrontation with EU bodies potentially aligned with incumbent vendors

Pursue a parallel-track EU engagement through academic and civil-society channels (EDRI, digital-rights networks, university research consortia) in phase one, building grassroots EU pressure independent of formal working-group channels, then fold this into official EU advocacy in phase three
review: Early working-group engagement gives maximum leverage but assumes the relevant EU bodies will prioritize vendor neutrality over incumbent relationships and smooth procurement processes. Late engagement preserves flexibility but may find AltID architecture already decided and European digital-identity standards locked in. Parallel civil-society pressure is insulating but creates two separate advocacy tracks that may contradict each other or dilute message authority if not carefully coordinated.

name: Demonstrator Feature Scope and Regulatory Realism Calibration
consequences: Defining the minimum viable scope for phase-two demonstrators (e.g., authentication only, no citizen data portability; no payment flows; no integration with legacy public systems) determines whether the work produces credible proof-of-concept or opens the project to criticism that fallback authentication is too limited to be practically useful. A narrow scope is defensible and delivers proof; a broader scope risks timeline slippage and gives opponents a target for dismissing the work as incomplete.
options: Limit phase-two demonstrators to single-factor FIDO2 passwordless authentication against a mock Danish identity provider, explicitly excluding multi-factor flows, attribute release, consent-management, or integration with downstream public services to keep engineering effort contained and timeline credible

Develop demonstrators covering passwordless authentication plus attribute selective disclosure ("give the hospital my emergency-contact info without revealing my tax ID") but exclude payment flows, biometric integration, and cross-border eIDAS interoperability to stay defensible while proving practical identity functionality

Build full-featured fallback demonstrators including passwordless auth, selective disclosure, payment-integration stubs, and simulated eIDAS interoperability with a partner EU member state, accepting longer timelines and external security review to prove that platform-neutral auth can support production-grade digital-identity use cases
review: Narrow scope is safe but provides ammunition for the claim that platform-neutral fallback is a theoretical curiosity, not a practical service replacement. Broader scope strengthens the feasibility argument but increases risk of missed timelines, failed security reviews, or discovering architectural blockers late in phase two, potentially triggering phase-three de-scoping. None of the options directly address the challenge that no real Danish citizen will actually use a non-production fallback demonstrator, so credibility rests entirely on technical design rigor and external expert review.

name: Incumbent Operator Transparency Negotiation and Information Access
consequences: Negotiating early and explicit access to MitID's platform-integrity APIs, app-distribution constraints, and telemetry dependencies either directly from the operator or via regulatory transparency request (GDPR Subject Access Request, FOIA, or targeted information demand) determines whether phase-one feasibility analysis is speculative or evidence-based. Full transparency accelerates the feasibility report and prevents the incumbent from later claiming the project's critique is technically uninformed; restricted access leaves gaps that incumbents can exploit to dismiss findings. However, transparency requests may trigger institutional defensiveness and accelerate incumbent opposition.
options: Request detailed information from the MitID operator under Danish FOIA and GDPR transparency provisions at month 2, explicitly framing the request as a resilience and continuity-planning exercise and offering a confidentiality agreement for sensitive architectural details, accepting the risk of triggering incumbent defensive mobilization

Approach the MitID operator's leadership directly at month 1 with a confidential briefing on the project's resilience-focused intent and request voluntary participation in technical interviews and architectural walkthrough sessions, prioritizing relationship-building over public pressure, accepting incomplete information in exchange for incumbent buy-in

Conduct the phase-one feasibility analysis using publicly available MitID documentation, published security research, and interviews with civil-sector actors and regulators who have visibility into MitID operations, publishing findings without direct operator input but also avoiding direct confrontation, leaving space for operator response and later engagement
review: Direct transparency requests accelerate evidence-based analysis but may weaponize the operator against the project if they perceive an implied sovereignty critique. Voluntary cooperation via relationship-building preserves incumbent goodwill but likely yields sanitized information and leaves architectural gaps that weaken the feasibility report. Relying on public documentation and third-party interviews is safest politically but may leave the project open to operator claims that its analysis is incomplete or tendentious, and the operator retains the ability to dismiss findings without having had the chance to respond to specific technical claims during the research phase.
